item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Department of Anomalous Drugs and Ordinance is sourced from this post by ihp, but as far as I know this is the only article that actually references it. The important thing is that this is either dado's backstory or his evil twin: the acronym is DADO.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoyed this, but I must ask.
Does The Department of Anomalous Drugs and Ordinance exist elsewhere on the Wiki, or did you just make it up as the anomalous branch of the ATF for this scip? I'm just curious if you've taken it from elsewhere that I can't seem to find, or if you got creative, because if it is the latter, I'm quite impressed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ spoilers within opinion
- spoilers within opinion

The two mysteries of this article — whether or not Josiah ascended to a higher plane and whether or not this plane actually exists or is just a figment of the believer’s imaginations — are bogged down by the fact that the answer to both is extraordinarily obvious and the fact that this article keeps resorting to them in order to try and push a shallow interpretation of the religion, rather than just diving further into the belief.

Due to the nature of the format, when I am presented with a question of whether or not something is anomalous or not, I am primed to say that it is.1 Why would it be included in a file about an anomaly if it didn’t have anything related to the anomalous? Therefore, when the Description first proposes this question, I’m able to answer it in half a second. This makes some later parts of the article painful to read because of the reliance on a mystery which was solved a thousand words ago.

Going into the first interview with this mindset, it’s… fine. It does its job, gives a brief description of what this realm is, and gets out. I do wish that Kollwitz would have described it using any other methods besides just spewing out whatever random adjectives and verbs came into his mind at the time so that I could actually understand what the place looked like, but it’s meant to be vague and cryptic.

The UIU log is very redundant. The description of the event was already told to me through the Description, so I gained little besides some more in-depth information. This does seem to be a problem more with the Description telling too much of the story early on than this log retreading on that territory.

I hate the final interview log. Besides the first half of it just being the UIU and Foundation interviewers saying, “Josiah didn’t go to heaven,” and Church saying, “Yeah, he did,” when I have already figured out the answer to that question, a lot of it is just so shallow. The angle of religion this looks at — it being a way for people to make sense of a chaotic and illogical world — has been done to death by the hundreds of other stories that talk about cults. That combined with the very blunt and cheap use of redactions in order to maintain some kind of mystery led me to have to work to keep my attention focused on the article.

This is a -1 from me.

Footnotes
1. The same way that I am primed to say the opposite for something like an Explained SCP.

item.title: About the format..
item.content:encoded:
Hey, can I use the format that you used

Like, this one.

+1, but seriously, Blackwire just came off as a tosser throughout the whole ordeal. I understand that you could become jaded throughout your career in the Foundation, but they were just unnecessarily rude throughout everything. Complete lack of professionalism, in my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Show Spoilers
- Hide Spoilers

Oh yeah, these guys were definitely worshipping pattern screamers. The repeated mention of patterns and screaming chaos gets me way too sus for it to be a coincidence. Which raises a chilling question: did Josiah really ascend, somehow? Did the screamers accept him into their nonexistence? Is he one of them now? Or has he simply been erased?

Maybe there’s no difference between the two…

Overall, I absolutely loved this article and the multi-faceted, multi-definition mystery it poses. Easy +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the concepts presented in this work, but (as some other folks have mentioned) the amount of text drags it down at times. I'm particularly concerned about the end of the description and the UIU log.

Novote for now, but I'm happy to upvote this after a round of editing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
soooo…

+ show block
– hide block

the manson family if Charlie had worshipped pattern screamers and been non violent?!

fun read. my finger slipped and I accidentally -1'd at first. was not my intention, and i fixed it, sorry. fun read… it'll be fun to think about in the coming days. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ spoiler
+ 1

>Department of Anomalous Drugs and Ordinance
shouldn't that be "department of anomalous drugs and ordinance"? kek

anyway, this is good +1 for patt screm, xcellent interview writing, and the nice pacing

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have some quibbles with the dialogue in the second interview and the paragraphs that introduce Thornettle, but I love how you've captured 60's LSD cults, and I love the Foundation's Most Wanted is this hippie with a frisbee. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was rad! I really enjoyed the idea of a hippy cult in the Foundationverse since so far all the other cults have been universally harmful and malevolent. Great change of pace. As for my theories:

++ Spoilers
Close

Feels like the higher plane could very well be where Ion went and the Sarkic entities live.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This has been strongly improved from when I reviewed it. The dialogue at the end is excellent and moving. However, I find it too long personally, and I think it doesn't fully capture the intent you were going for. I think it could be much stronger than it is now, but it's by no means bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wasn't blown away, and I found Agent Blackwire's lack of professionalism annoying at points, but overall I enjoyed this as a meditation on the idea of faith and the clash between the searches for knowledge vs enlightenment. Good character work on the cult members in particular, and the idea behind the redactions, if McB is correct, adds another strong element (and helps me understand Blackwire a bit more, perhaps).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I loved this as a draft and I love it now.

+ Spoilers
– hide block

The idea of a religion that develops around the worship of pattern screamers (at least, that's what I've decided those "angels" closely resemble) is friggin' awesome, and you have some real vivid imagery present throughout.


Easy +1 for the excellent story and the unique take on the contest theme.

item.title: A Brief Moment of Revelation
item.content:encoded:
Here it is, my 5k. This was intended to mostly be about one of the weirder definitions of mystery - specifically, "a religious belief based on divine revelation, especially one regarded as beyond human understanding" - but then a regular mystery snuck back in with the idea of Thornettle disappearing without a trace. They're still linked, but I was really expecting this to only be tangentially linked to the theme. Funny how it works out.

Thanks to my reviewers SoullessSingularity, TheMightyMcB, ManyMeats, Popsioak, JakdragonX, MirageTD and Habitual_Snubnose for loooking this over before posting.

The Mysteries Herewithin
How ... mysterious
  1. The first mystery is the sacred mystery, under the definition above. It is the mystery of faith, but also the theme of divine enlightenment and things that can only be understood through that lense. This is not a mystery in the typical sense, and has no solution.
  2. The second mystery is what happened to Thornettle. Did he burn to death, get fatally [REDACTED] in another dimension, join the angels, or has he escaped the UIU/SCP all these years? This is not a mystery that innately has an answer within the article.
  3. The third mystery is what is behind the [REDACTED]s. This has an answer within the SCP and can be solved. Please put any guesses to this in spoilers in the comments.
cameos and trivia
random tidbits
  • The Department of Anomalous Drugs and Ordinance is said to be severely understaffed. This is because they are only a single man. Many years after this article, something happens to this man, and DADO becomes dado.
  • The friend from Reed College actually went to Deer College and had done other anomalous drugs before, so the stuff they did with Kollwitz didn't knock their socks off. They just told Kollwitz they went to Reed, which was close enough to the truth.

Image Sources:

  • Thornettle.jpg (CC 3.0)
  • higherlove.jpg (CC 2.0. Originally Untitled by Nicholas Darinzo, desaturated by myself)
  • angel.jpg (CC 4.0. Originally by user Polly Glott.)
  • uiu.png was created by Zhange and released under Creative Commons. I altered the image to desaturate it.
